ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 133935, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src"."ansprechpartner" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner  (201 rows)

Columns:
- `ap_id` text NOT NULL
- `vorname` text NULL
- `nachname` text NULL
- `email_adresse` text NULL
- `telefonnummer` text NULL
- `position` text NULL
- `funktion` text NULL
- `sprache` text NULL
- `kunde` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.assets  (400 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_id` text NOT NULL
- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `seriennr` text NULL
- `garantie_bis` text NULL
- `kd_ref` text NULL
- `projekt_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.chancen  (235 rows)

Columns:
- `chance_id` text NOT NULL
- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `phase` text NULL
- `abschlussdatum` text NULL
- `volumen` double precision NULL
- `waehrung` text NULL
- `kd_nr` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden  (100 rows)

Columns:
- `kunden_nr` text NOT NULL
- `firmenname` text NULL
- `erp_nummer` text NULL
- `kategorie` text NULL
- `gebiet` text NULL
- `branche` text NULL
- `webseite` text NULL
- `ort` text NULL
- `land` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.proj  (155 rows)

Columns:
- `proj_id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `kd` text NULL
- `opp` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: after writing models, call run_dbt(), fix any errors, and read back every model file you edit before finishing.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 133944, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 134311, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62ba-e054-770c-a9d5-0f42c7b10bb3', conversation_id='019f62ba-e054-770c-a9d5-0f419a5a9a23')

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content="Now I have a clear picture of the source data. Here are the key observations:

1. **Key Relationships**:
   - `kunden.kunden_nr` is referenced as `kunde` in `ansprechpartner`, `kd_nr` in `chancen`, `kd` in `proj`, and `kd_ref` in `assets`.
   - `ansprechpartner.ap_id` is the contact ID.
   - `chancen.chance_id` is the opportunity ID.
   - `proj.proj_id` is the project ID.
   - `assets.asset_id` is the asset ID.
   - `proj.opp` references `chancen.chance_id`.

2. **Date Formats**: Dates in `abschlussdatum`, `go_live`, and `garantie_bis` are already in `YYYY-MM-DD` format.

3. **Enum Mappings**:
   - `kunden.kategorie` maps to `Account.Customer_Tier__c` (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum).
   - `ansprechpartner.funktion` maps to `Contact.Role__c` (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor).
   - `ansprechpartner.sprache` maps to `Contact.Preferred_Language__c` (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT).
   - `chancen.phase` maps to `Opportunity.StageName`.
   - `proj.status` maps to `Project__c.Project_Status__c`.

4. **Salesforce-style IDs**:
   - The target tables require `Id` columns (Salesforce-style IDs). The source tables have IDs like `CUST-1001`, `CON-00001`, etc. We need to map these to Salesforce-style IDs. Since there's no direct mapping, we'll use the source IDs as the `Id` for now, but ensure `AccountId` and `Account__c` reference the `Account.Id` (i.e., the `kunden_nr` mapped to `Account.Id`).

5. **Legacy Columns**:
   - `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` → `kunden.kunden_nr`
   - `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` → `ansprechpartner.ap_id`
   - `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` → `chancen.chance_id`
   - `Legacy_Project_ID__c` → `proj.proj_id`
   - `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` → `assets.asset_id`

6. **Currency Handling**: `chancen.volumen` is already `double precision`, and `waehrung` is the currency code.

7. **Default Values for NOT NULL Columns**:
   - `CreatedDate` and `LastModifiedDate` can be set to `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` or a default like `NULL` (but the target spec says `text` for these columns, so we'll use `NULL` as a string or a timestamp string).
   - `IsDeleted` is an integer (0 or 1), default to 0.

---

### Step 1: Create `sources.yml`"), ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args='{"path": "models/sources.yml", "content": "version: 2\n\nsources:\n  - name: fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src\n    schema: \"fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src\"\n    tables:\n      - name: kunden\n      - name: ansprechpartner\n      - name: chancen\n      - name: proj\n      - name: assets\n"}', tool_call_id='RbEEXWGQZ')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=5450, output_tokens=762), model_name='nvidia/mistral-medium-3.5-128b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 27, 48, 927792, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 26, 34, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-b689873d56a81e61', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 133935, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src"."ansprechpartner" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner  (201 rows)

Columns:
- `ap_id` text NOT NULL
- `vorname` text NULL
- `nachname` text NULL
- `email_adresse` text NULL

## Project__c

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:

- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content="Now I have a clear picture of the source data. Here are the key observations:

1. **Key Relationships**:
   - `kunden.kunden_nr` is referenced as `kunde` in `ansprechpartner`, `kd_nr` in `chancen`, `kd` in `proj`, and `kd_ref` in `assets`.
   - `ansprechpartner.ap_id` is the contact ID.
   - `chancen.chance_id` is the opportunity ID.
   - `proj.proj_id` is the project ID.
   - `assets.asset_id` is the asset ID.
   - `proj.opp` references `chancen.chance_id`.

2. **Date Formats**: Dates in `abschlussdatum`, `go_live`, and `garantie_bis` are already in `YYYY-MM-DD` format.

3. **Enum Mappings**:
   - `kunden.kategorie` maps to `Account.Customer_Tier__c` (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum).
   - `ansprechpartner.funktion` maps to `Contact.Role__c` (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor).
   - `ansprechpartner.sprache` maps to `Contact.Preferred_Language__c` (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT).
   - `chancen.phase` maps to `Opportunity.StageName`.
   - `proj.status` maps to `Project__c.Project_Status__c`.

4. **Salesforce-style IDs**:
   - The target tables require `Id` columns (Salesforce-style IDs). The source tables have IDs like `CUST-1001`, `CON-00001`, etc. We need to map these to Salesforce-style IDs. Since there's no direct mapping, we'll use the source IDs as the `Id` for now, but ensure `AccountId` and `Account__c` reference the `Account.Id` (i.e., the `kunden_nr` mapped to `Account.Id`).

5. **Legacy Columns**:
   - `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` → `kunden.kunden_nr`
   - `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` → `ansprechpartner.ap_id`
   - `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` → `chancen.chance_id`
   - `Legacy_Project_ID__c` → `proj.proj_id`
   - `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` → `assets.asset_id`

6. **Currency Handling**: `chancen.volumen` is already `double precision`, and `waehrung` is the currency code.

7. **Default Values for NOT NULL Columns**:
   - `CreatedDate` and `LastModifiedDate` can be set to `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` or a default like `NULL` (but the target spec says `text` for these columns, so we'll use `NULL` as a string or a timestamp string).
   - `IsDeleted` is an integer (0 or 1), default to 0.

---

### Step 1: Create `sources.yml`"), ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args='{"path": "models/sources.yml", "content": "version: 2\n\nsources:\n  - name: fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src\n    schema: \"fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src\"\n    tables:\n      - name: kunden\n      - name: ansprechpartner\n      - name: chancen\n      - name: proj\n      - name: assets\n"}', tool_call_id='RbEEXWGQZ')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=5450, output_tokens=762), model_name='nvidia/mistral-medium-3.5-128b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 27, 48, 927792, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 26, 34, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-b689873d56a81e61', finish_reason='tool_call', run_id='019f62ba-e054-770c-a9d5-0f42c7b10bb3', conversation_id='019f62ba-e054-770c-a9d5-0f419a5a9a23')

response: ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content='### Step 2: Create the `Account` Model The `Account` table maps from `kunden`. The `Id` will be the `kunden_nr` (e.g., `CUST-1001`). `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` is also `kunden_nr`. `Customer_Tier__c` maps from `kategorie`. `Region__c` maps from `gebiet`. `Industry` maps from `branche`. `Website` maps from `webseite`. `BillingCity` maps from `ort`. `BillingCountry` maps from `land`. `ERP_Number__c` maps from `erp_nummer`. `CreatedDate` and `LastModifiedDate` will be set to `NULL` (or a default timestamp string). `IsDeleted` defaults to 0.'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args='{"path": "models/Account.sql", "content": "{{ config(materialized=\'table\') }}\\n\\nSELECT\\n    kunden_nr AS \\"Id\\",\\n    firmenname AS \\"Name\\",\\n    erp_nummer AS \\"ERP_Number__c\\",\\n    CASE \\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(kategorie)) IN (\'GOLD\', \'SILVER\', \'BRONZE\', \'PLATINUM\') THEN INITCAP(LOWER(TRIM(kategorie)))\\n        ELSE NULL\\n    END AS \\"Customer_Tier__c\\",\\n    gebiet AS \\"Region__c\\",\\n    branche AS \\"Industry\\",\\n    webseite AS \\"Website\\",\\n    ort AS \\"BillingCity\\",\\n    land AS \\"BillingCountry\\",\\n    kunden_nr AS \\"Legacy_Customer_ID__c\\",\\n    NULL AS \\"CreatedDate\\",\\n    NULL AS \\"LastModifiedDate\\",\\n    0 AS \\"IsDeleted\\"\\nFROM {{ source(\'fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src\', \'kunden\') }}\\n"}', tool_call_id='LNKTEzJLH')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=6214, output_tokens=420), model_name='nvidia/mistral-medium-3.5-128b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 28, 19, 673612, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 27, 59, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-a5a22c802ca5b68e', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 133935, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src"."ansprechpartner" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner  (201 rows)

Columns:
- `ap_id` text NOT NULL
- `vorname` text NULL
- `nachname` text NULL
- `email_adresse` text NULL
- `telefonnummer` text NULL
- `position` text NULL
- `funktion` text NULL
- `sprache` text NULL
- `kunde` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.assets  (400 rows)

- `seriennr` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.chancen  (235 rows)

Columns:

- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `phase` text NULL

- `waehrung` text NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden  (100 rows)

- `gebiet` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 133935, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src"."ansprechpartner" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner  (201 rows)

Columns:
- `ap_id` text NOT NULL
- `vorname` text NULL
- `nachname` text NULL
- `email_adresse` text NULL
- `telefonnummer` text NULL
- `position` text NULL
- `funktion` text NULL
- `sprache` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

- `abschlussdatum` text NULL
- `volumen` double precision NULL

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden  (100 rows)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL

- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: after writing models, call run_dbt(), fix any errors, and read back every model file you edit before finishing.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 133944, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 134311, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62ba-e054-770c-a9d5-0f42c7b10bb3', conversation_id='019f62ba-e054-770c-a9d5-0f419a5a9a23')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 133935, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src"."ansprechpartner" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner  (201 rows)

Columns:
- `ap_id` text NOT NULL
- `vorname` text NULL
- `nachname` text NULL
- `email_adresse` text NULL
- `telefonnummer` text NULL
- `position` text NULL
- `funktion` text NULL
- `sprache` text NULL
- `kunde` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging
- (optionally) run_dbt() — execute the dbt project and see the result

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- You MUST call run_dbt() after writing or editing models and before declaring the project complete.
- If run_dbt() reports errors, read the failing model files, fix the SQL, and call run_dbt() again until it succeeds or you cannot fix the errors.
- Do NOT skip run_dbt() to save time — the orchestrator will run dbt again and fail if SQL is invalid.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 133935, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src"."ansprechpartner" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.ansprechpartner  (201 rows)

Columns:
- `ap_id` text NOT NULL
- `vorname` text NULL
- `nachname` text NULL
- `email_adresse` text NULL
- `telefonnummer` text NULL
- `position` text NULL
- `funktion` text NULL
- `sprache` text NULL
- `kunde` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.assets  (400 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_id` text NOT NULL
- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `seriennr` text NULL
- `garantie_bis` text NULL
- `kd_ref` text NULL
- `projekt_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.chancen  (235 rows)

Columns:
- `chance_id` text NOT NULL
- `bezeichnung` text NULL
- `phase` text NULL
- `abschlussdatum` text NULL
- `volumen` double precision NULL
- `waehrung` text NULL
- `kd_nr` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.kunden  (100 rows)

Columns:
- `kunden_nr` text NOT NULL
- `firmenname` text NULL
- `erp_nummer` text NULL
- `kategorie` text NULL
- `gebiet` text NULL
- `branche` text NULL
- `webseite` text NULL

- `land` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src.proj  (155 rows)

Columns:
- `proj_id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `kd` text NULL
- `opp` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: after writing models, call run_dbt(), fix any errors, and read back every model file you edit before finishing.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 133944, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 24, 2, 134311, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f62ba-e054-770c-a9d5-0f42c7b10bb3', conversation_id='019f62ba-e054-770c-a9d5-0f419a5a9a23')

The `Id` will be the `asset_id` (e.g., `AST-00001`). `Account__c` must reference the `Account.Id` (i.e., the `kd_ref` field in `assets` maps to `kunden.kunden_nr`, which is `Account.Id`). `Project__c` must reference the `Project__c.Id` (i.e., the `projekt_ref` field in `assets` maps to `proj.proj_id`, which is `Project__c.Id`). `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` is `asset_id`. `Serial_Number__c` maps from `seriennr`. `Warranty_End_Date__c` maps from `garantie_bis`. `CreatedDate` and `LastModifiedDate` are set to `NULL`. `IsDeleted` defaults to 0.'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args='{"path": "models/Installed_Asset__c.sql", "content": "{{ config(materialized=\'table\') }}\\n\\nSELECT\\n    a.asset_id AS \\"Id\\",\\n    a.bezeichnung AS \\"Name\\",\\n    a.seriennr AS \\"Serial_Number__c\\",\\n    a.garantie_bis AS \\"Warranty_End_Date__c\\",\\n    k.kunden_nr AS \\"Account__c\\",\\n    p.proj_id AS \\"Project__c\\",\\n    a.asset_id AS \\"Legacy_Asset_ID__c\\",\\n    NULL AS \\"CreatedDate\\",\\n    NULL AS \\"LastModifiedDate\\",\\n    0 AS \\"IsDeleted\\"\\nFROM {{ source(\'fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src\', \'assets\') }} a\\nLEFT JOIN {{ source(\'fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src\', \'kunden\') }} k ON a.kd_ref = k.kunden_nr\\nLEFT JOIN {{ source(\'fixture_wrong_field_names_v2_src\', \'proj\') }} p ON a.projekt_ref = p.proj_id\\n"}', tool_call_id='R7iZtIbOK')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=8558, output_tokens=462), model_name='nvidia/mistral-medium-3.5-128b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 34, 28, 449039, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 14, 22, 33, 4, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-96048b101847f563', finish_reason='tool_call', run_id='019f62ba-e054-770c-a9d5-0f42c7b10bb3', conversation_id='019f62ba-e054-770c-a9d5-0f419a5a9a23')
